description: A 'phao' is a parent through a continuing relation to a child. The relation may come through birth or accepted caregiving. Adoption belongs here; 'thowia phao' (birth parent) makes the biological source explicit when it matters. Parenting itself is said through acts such as 'numelo' (nurture) and 'theama' (care).
articulatory_notes: Bring the lips close without closing them and push unvoiced air through the narrow opening for 'ph'. Lower the tongue into stressed open central 'a', then keep middle-height back rounded 'o' in a separate syllable across the hiatus.
sound_symbolism: The fricative begins as breath between nearly closed lips, then both vowels remain open and distinct. That movement from held air into two clear syllables gives the word a gentle spaciousness without assigning care to either sound.
usage_notes: The unmarked noun leaves gender unstated. A possessor before 'phao' identifies whose parent is meant, while the surrounding clause states the care, descent, or sourced status under discussion.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The unmarked form lets a speaker name care or descent without assigning a gender class. The next verb says what the parent actually did.

semantic_domains.community: a continuing family relation to a child
